message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-9). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope examples: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts; on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY P9-MO-2.

FINDING P9-MO-2 (low): Bare int()/float() env parsing crashes import with opaque error in seam.py/checkpoint.py
Location: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py + persistence/checkpoint.py (env int/float parsing)
Claim: seam.py and checkpoint.py parse numeric env vars with bare int()/float(), so a malformed value raises an opaque ValueError at import/use with no indication of WHICH env var — the exact class already fixed elsewhere via _int_env (tracing.py, vectorstore.py, per #H-022/#CV-S-1). VERIFY the bare int()/float() calls exist on env values and are not wrapped to name the offending var.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me look at the `_env` helper in seam.py and the existing `_int_env` helpers to understand the difference.

75	    """Rate-limit / capacity signal — retried with long backpressure, not fast backoff."""

79	# Config (dataset-agnostic env contract, ASSAY_* prefix)

95	# Cap BULK generation length. ChatOllama/Ollama default to unbounded (-1); without a cap a
96	# local model can run away to context exhaustion, and the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a
97	# call already blocked in the backend (a leaked pool slot). Bounding output tokens is the real
98	# guard (LangChain's own ChatOllama example sets num_predict explicitly).
99	BULK_NUM_PREDICT = max(1, int(_env("ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT", "2048")))

105	RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_RETRIES", "5")))
106	# run_json re-rolls on a PARSE failure. This is independent of the transient/rate-limit retry
107	# budget above (which applies per generation) so the two do not multiply into a retry storm; an
108	# overall wall-clock deadline bounds the total regardless (#102).
109	JSON_REROLLS = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS", "2")))
110	RUN_JSON_DEADLINE = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE", "600")))

112	# OAuth subscription token name (external CLI contract — not renamed).
113	OAUTH_TOKEN_ENV = "CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN"
114	
115	
116	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
117	# Credential scrubbing (ADR-0003 / no metered API) — pure, forward-proof
118	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
119	def is_metered_anthropic_credential(key: str) -> bool:
120	    """True for any env var the Anthropic SDK would read as a METERED credential.
121	
122	    Matches the *shape* — any ``ANTHROPIC_*`` var naming a ``KEY`` or ``TOKEN`` — rather than
123	    a static list, so a newly-added metered var cannot silently slip through. The subscription
124	    ``CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`` does not start with ``ANTHROPIC_`` and is deliberately kept.
125	
126	    The match is case-insensitive (pass 3, #F-003): a metered key set under a non-uppercase name
127	    (``anthropic_api_key``) must scrub too — the AWS_/GOOGLE_ checks already uppercase, so this
128	    closes the one credential path that did not.
129	    """
130	    ku = key.upper()
131	    return ku.startswith("ANTHROPIC_") and ("KEY" in ku or "TOKEN" in ku)
132	
133	
134	# Env vars that — beyond metered credentials — would redirect the subprocess OFF-BOX or onto a
135	# metered provider, defeating ADR-0003 / "no metered API". The claude CLI honours all of these.
136	_UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS = frozenset(
137	    {
138	        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK",
139	        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX",
140	        "CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER",
141	        "GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS",
142	        # TLS-trust / egress-interception knobs the Node-based CLI honours: a custom CA or disabled
143	        # cert verification enables a transparent off-box MITM proxy (pass 4, #G-018).
144	        "NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS",
145	        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED",
146	    }
147	)
148	
149	
150	def is_unsafe_subprocess_var(key: str) -> bool:
151	    """True for any env var that must not reach the high-stakes subprocess (#101).
152	
153	    Covers metered credentials, off-box/metered-provider redirects, proxies (which redirect
154	    egress), cloud-provider credentials (AWS_*/GOOGLE_*), and TLS-trust overrides. The ENTIRE
155	    ``ANTHROPIC_*`` namespace is scrubbed (#G-018): any such var configures the SDK's endpoint /
156	    auth / headers and could redirect off-box or onto a metered provider — the subscription token
157	    is ``CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`` (no ``ANTHROPIC_`` prefix) and is deliberately NOT matched.
158	    """
159	    ku = key.upper()
160	    return (
161	        ku.startswith(
162	            "ANTHROPIC_"
163	        )  # whole namespace: endpoint/auth/headers/custom-headers (#G-018)
164	        or ku in _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS
165	        or ku.endswith("_PROXY")  # HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any case)
166	        or ku.startswith("AWS_")
167	        or ku.startswith("GOOGLE_")
168	    )
169	
170	
171	def scrubbed_env() -> dict[str, str]:
172	    """The process env hardened for the high-stakes subprocess (ADR-0003 / no metered API).
173	
174	    Every unsafe var (:func:`is_unsafe_subprocess_var`) is **overwritten to empty**, not merely
175	    omitted. The claude-agent-sdk builds the child env as ``{**os.environ, **options.env}`` — it
176	    *merges* over the inherited environment — so a dropped key would silently fall through to its
177	    inherited value (the metered key / off-box redirect would survive). Setting it to ``""`` in
178	    the dict we hand over guarantees the merge overwrites the inherited value (#101). The
179	    subscription OAuth token is preserved so the fixed-cost path still authenticates.
180	    """
181	    env = dict(os.environ)
182	    for k in list(env):
183	        if is_unsafe_subprocess_var(k):
184	            env[k] = ""
185	    return env
186	
187	
188	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
189	# Bounded timeout pool with saturation guard

28	# Local collector endpoint (loopback-enforced).
29	TRACING_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_HOST", "localhost")
30	
31	
32	def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
33	    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).
34	
35	    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
36	    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
37	    """
38	    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
39	    try:
40	        return int(raw)
41	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
42	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
43	
44	
45	TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
46	PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")

48	_provider: Any = None
49	# Guards the _provider check-then-set so concurrent bootstrap_tracing() calls don't race.
50	_provider_lock = threading.Lock()
51	
52	
53	def _disabled() -> bool:
54	    return os.environ.get("ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
55	
56	
57	def tracing_base_url() -> str:
58	    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
59	    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"
60	
61	
62	def tracing_endpoint() -> str:
63	    """OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint at the local collector (loopback-enforced)."""
64	    return f"{tracing_base_url()}/v1/traces"
65	
66	
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:

55	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
56	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
57	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
58	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
59	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
60	# tests monkeypatch.
61	_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
62	
63	
64	def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
65	    """The advisory-lock wait bound, re-read from the env each call (#F-033).
66	
67	    Falls back to the import-time module constant when the env var is unset, so a test that
68	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
69	    """
70	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
71	    if raw is None:
72	        return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
73	    return max(0, int(raw))
74	
75	
76	# Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
77	# list, the per-conn lock map, the pool cache). It is held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set — it
78	# is NEVER held across blocking cross-process I/O (pool.connection(), the advisory-lock wait, or
79	# setup() DDL). That blocking work is serialized per conn_str instead (#129), so a stuck advisory

content.text: The finding is confirmed by inspection. The `_env()` helper in seam.py only handles default substitution and returns a string; the surrounding `int()`/`float()` calls are bare. Let me reproduce the opaque error to confirm.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=abc python -c "import assay_engine.reasoning.seam" 2>&1 | tail -5; echo "=== checkpoint ==="; ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=oops python -c "import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint" 2>&1 | tail -5
input.description: Reproduce opaque ValueError on malformed env at import

content.content:
)

    BULK_NUM_PREDICT = max(1, int(_env("ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT", "2048")))
                              ~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'
=== checkpoint ===
    )
  File "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py", line 61, in <module>
    _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
                                        ~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'oops'

content.text:
Reproduced. Both modules crash at import with an opaque `invalid literal for int() with base 10` error. The traceback does name the line (and thus the var is visible in the source line), but the exception message itself does not name the offending env var — exactly the class fixed elsewhere via `_int_env` in tracing.py/vectorstore.py, which raises `f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}"`.

Note the checkpoint.py case crashes at *import time* (line 61, module level), which is the worst form. The seam.py bulk constants also crash at import. This is an inconsistency with the established `_int_env` pattern in the same codebase.

The finding's claim is accurate: the bare `int()`/`float()` calls exist on env values, they are not wrapped to name the offending var, and the exact mitigation pattern (`_int_env`) already exists elsewhere in the project for this same class.

input.reasoning:
Verified by code inspection and reproduction on branch harden/pass-9.

seam.py (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py) wraps env values with bare int()/float() at module-import scope: line 94 BULK_TIMEOUT=float(_env(...)), line 99 BULK_NUM_PREDICT=int(_env(...)), lines 100/102-105/109-110 similar. Its _env() helper (lines 81-83) only substitutes a default and returns a string; it does NOT validate or name the var on malformed input.

checkpoint.py (src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py) has the same pattern: line 61 _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=max(0,int(os.environ.get(...))) at import scope, line 73 int(raw), lines 271-273 pool-size ints — all bare int() on env values.

Reproduced the opaque crash:
- ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=abc python -c "import assay_engine.reasoning.seam" -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc' (seam.py:99)
- ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=oops python -c "import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint" -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'oops' (checkpoint.py:61)

The exception MESSAGE names neither the env var nor offers a clear contract — exactly the class already fixed elsewhere. tracing.py:32-43 and vectorstore.py:20 define _int_env(name, default) that catches ValueError/TypeError and re-raises f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}", explicitly citing #CV-S-1 / #H-022. seam.py and checkpoint.py do not use that helper, so they remain inconsistent and emit the opaque error this finding describes. Both crash at import time (worst case for diagnosability).

Not out-of-scope: this is a genuine operator-ergonomics defect, the same class the team already deemed worth fixing, and the helper to apply already exists. TRUE_POSITIVE (low severity, as stated).

content.text: Verdict delivered: TRUE_POSITIVE for P9-MO-2. The finding is confirmed by both inspection and reproduction — bare `int()`/`float()` calls on env values in `src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py` and `src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` crash at import with an opaque `invalid literal for int()` error that does not name the offending env var, while the project already has the `_int_env` mitigation pattern (tracing.py, vectorstore.py) for exactly this class.
